event.name: Masters Tournament
event.course_notes: Thinker's course. Course management is a genuine differentiating skill. Distance on 13 and 15 specifically — bomber advantage real. Putting rewards course-specific understanding not generic Tour metrics. Experience weight is higher here than almost anywhere else. ARG and angles around greens matter enormously. Know where to miss and where NOT to miss on every hole. First timers get meaningful newcomer discount even with elite metrics.

analysis.briefing_paragraph: Augusta National is the most unforgiving course on tour for punishing wrong answers. The Angle Penalty is HIGH for a reason — this is not a course where you spray it and recover. Every approach has a preferred window, and the players who win here repeatedly aren't just ball-strikers, they're *thinkers* who understand that laying up at 13 or 15 at the right moment is as valuable as going for it in the right moment. The dominant stat profile here is genuinely balanced — you need OTT quality to access the par-5s (13 and 15 in particular reward distance meaningfully), you need iron precision to hit the correct quadrants of these greens rather than just the putting surface, and you need Augusta-specific putting competency that doesn't translate from generic Tour SG:PUTT numbers. The rough penalty being LOW doesn't mean missing fairways is free — it means the penalty comes from angles and positions, not from vegetation. That's actually harder to model and easier to underestimate in prep. The confirmed result — Rory McIlroy defending his Masters title — is historically significant and framework-relevant. Defending at Augusta is one of the hardest things in golf. The course's premium on course knowledge and management experience is supposed to help veterans, but the field also has that same knowledge. What McIlroy apparently demonstrated over four rounds, including overcoming what sounds like a difficult start in the final round ("horror start" per the headline) and a six-shot lead he'd surrendered, is elite course management under pressure combined with the ball-striking engine to back it up. That's the Augusta profile working exactly as the framework predicts: T2G as the floor, clutch management as the ceiling. The fact that he clawed back after a horror start and still won tells me his APP game held up when it mattered — you don't recover at Augusta on putts alone. ---

S.player: Profile: Strong ball-striking metrics, multiple Augusta appearances, no obvious weakness that Augusta punishes. These are the players who should have been in contention Sunday.

Profile: One or two Augusta-specific strengths but a question mark somewhere. Maybe elite driver, average iron play. Or elite irons, limited Augusta experience. Interesting at the right price, not a primary target.
Profile: Metrics don't fit the course type, limited experience, or recent form trending wrong direction.

FADE.player: Profile: Generic putting spike driving SG total (market trap). Players whose T2G is mediocre but a hot putter has inflated their recent results. Augusta putts don't transfer from other courses. This is the primary market trap at this event every year.

Rory McIlroy** *(confirmed winner of the 2026 Masters)*
Experience weight at Augusta is not overrated in the framework
T2G engine players survive Augusta adversity (he overcame a horror start in R4)
### AVAILABLE PLAYS (Framework-Based Pool Guidance for Future Reference)
Use in pool:** Players with the full Augusta profile

Secondary pool targets:** Players who finished T5 or better and showed the correct ball-striking profile. Without leaderboard data I can't name them, but the framework says: whoever was in the final pairing or within 3 shots with 18 holes to play almost certainly has the Augusta-fit profile worth pooling going forward.
### TRAP PLAYS TO AVOID IN POOL
🚫 **Players with Augusta history of Amen Corner disasters**
All 50 player slots returned "Unknown / N/A" across every metric. This is not a data gap
Six sources were entirely inaccessible (Haslbauer, Steve Rawlings, Dave Tindall, Tom Jacobs, Sharpside/Gannon

This is flagged as a Sunday Night pre-analysis, but the articles confirm the tournament has concluded (McIlroy wins headline, "Three Sunday takeaways" piece referenced). The field and odds data not yet arriving is unusual given the tournament is over. This may be a workflow timing issue where the analysis framework is running but the correct data feed hasn't populated yet. Recommend confirming whether this is meant to be a post-tournament debrief or if there's a data pipeline issue.
The Bamford and Coley articles that did process were titled "RBC Heritage Tips 2026"

The framework historically applies a slight discount to defending champions because the field knows their game, they face elevated scrutiny/media pressure, and complacency is a real factor. Augusta specifically may be an exception to this rule. The course knowledge advantage for a defending champion here is more durable than at other events because the knowledge is so course-specific. I'd adjust the framework to apply a *smaller* defending champion discount at Augusta than at standard Tour events.

The Sporting Life headline notes McIlroy overcame a "horror start" in R4. This is worth flagging as a framework data point: elite ball-strikers at Augusta can absorb early adversity and recover because the course has opportunities (par-5s) to make up ground in clusters. A player with a bad front nine isn't dead at Augusta the way they might be at a tight, penalizing course. This reinforces the T2G engine > putting spike framework specifically here.

McIlroy entered Sunday with a six-shot lead and surrendered it before winning. This is a significant narrative that I can't fully analyze without knowing the full leaderboard, but it confirms that Augusta specifically creates conditions where leads evaporate due to course chaos (Amen Corner, par-5 gamble decisions, etc.). For future betting: having a six-shot lead at Augusta entering Sunday is less safe than anywhere else. This is a live-betting calibration note.
When data arrives Monday, here's the rebuild sequence:
**Get actual player field list with stats**

This analysis reflects the maximum available output given a complete field data failure and 12 blocked articles. The framework is intact. The data pipeline needs to be checked before Monday's full build.*

analysis_uncertainties: [Claude] ## ⚠️ CRITICAL DATA FLAGS — READ BEFORE ANALYSIS

[Claude] Before I get into anything, I have to be completely transparent about what I'm working with here, because the data situation on this one is significantly compromised and I need to flag it loudly rather than paper over it.
[Claude] **What went wrong this week:**
[Claude] - All six primary analytical articles blocked (403/404 errors) — Haslbauer (x2), Rawlings, Tindall, Jacobs, Gannon

[Claude] - The two articles that DID process were effectively useless for this event: Bamford's piece was RBC Heritage content (wrong tournament entirely), and Coley's piece returned navigation/homepage text only — no analytical substance extracted
[Claude] - **The entire field data table came back as 50 rows of "Unknown / N/A"** — no player names, no SG data, no DG win percentages, no designations
[Claude] - No prior year course breakdown content survived (player notes discarded per rules, structural notes appear empty)
[Claude] - The course setup parameters you provided

[Claude] - The Ben Coley homepage snippet, which contains one genuinely important headline: **"McIlroy wins the Masters again"** — Rory McIlroy defended his Masters title in 2026, overcoming a horror start and a host of challengers
[Claude] - My trained framework knowledge of Augusta National
[Claude] - My trained knowledge of the field through my training window

[Claude] **My approach given these constraints:** I'm going to give you a full, honest briefing using framework-grounded Augusta knowledge plus the confirmed result, clearly labeled throughout. I'll flag everywhere I'm operating without current data. This is NOT a situation where I fill in the blanks silently — you get what I actually have and nothing more.
[Claude] ## 1. BRIEFING PARAGRAPH

[Claude] Augusta National is the most experience-weighted course on the calendar and I treat it that way every single year. The setup parameters confirm what I already know: NEUTRAL course type, BALANCED dominant stat, LOW rough penalty, HIGH angle penalty, and a Distance Multiplier of PARTIAL. That last point matters — distance is real here, specifically on 13 and 15 where bombers can take the par-5s in two with irons while shorter hitters are forced into awkward layup decisions, but distance without management is a route to disaster. The HIGH angle penalty is the number that actually drives my thinking at Augusta more than anything else. Where your ball ends up — not just whether it finds the fairway — determines your scoring opportunity. A player 20 yards offline in the right direction is in business. The same player 20 yards offline in the wrong direction is making bogey or worse. That's why SG:APP is my primary driver here, but it has to be APP that's directionally aware, not just aggregate proximity. The putting surface at Augusta rewards course-specific knowledge accumulated over multiple visits — generic Tour putting metrics are among the most misleading inputs at this event. First-timers get a meaningful discount in my framework regardless of their metrics. Experience is a real asset here, not a narrative crutch.
[Claude] **Tournament Context (2026):**

[Claude] The headline coming out of this week is significant: Rory McIlroy has now defended his Masters title, becoming just the fourth player in history to win back-to-back Masters. The Coley snippet notes he overcame a "horror start" and "a host of challengers" — which tells me the tournament wasn't a wire-to-wire runaway, there was real chaos involved, and Rory found a way through it anyway. That's a mentally and historically important data point. The fact that he surrendered a six-shot lead heading into Sunday (per the R4 preview note) and still won tells you something about the depth of his Augusta course management and his closing ability under pressure. From a forward-looking framework standpoint, McIlroy is now a two-time defending champion going into 2027, which creates an interesting tension: does that create even more market overreaction toward him next year, or does the history of Augusta (which has been notoriously unkind to three-peats) start working against his price? I'm flagging that now, twelve months early, because it will matter.
[Claude] ## 2. INITIAL PLAYER RANKINGS
[Claude] ### ⚠️ IMPORTANT CAVEAT

[Claude] The field data table was entirely blank — 50 rows of Unknown/N/A. I cannot produce a data-driven ranking this week. What I can do is give you my Augusta-framework-grounded tier structure based on what I know about the course fit profiles, and flag it clearly as framework/knowledge-based rather than current-data-driven. Treat this as a structural reference, not a live analysis. Odds and current form data need to come in before this becomes actionable.
[Claude] ### AUGUSTA NATIONAL — COURSE FIT PROFILE
[Claude] *What the course actually demands, in priority order:*
[Claude] 1. **SG:APP (directional, not just aggregate)** — The engine. Angle penalty is HIGH, which means approach quality and angle management is the true differentiator.
[Claude] 2. **Experience / Course History** — Heavier weight here than anywhere else. Multi-time participants understand where to miss, what pins to attack, what to leave below the hole.
[Claude] 3. **SG:OTT (distance + accuracy balance)** — Distance matters on 13/15. Wild OTT players pay for it on the angled approach holes. Can't be one-dimensional here.
[Claude] 4. **ARG (around-the-green scrambling)** — Augusta's collection areas and chip shot variety are among the most demanding on Tour. This is a quiet differentiator.

[Claude] 5. **SG:PUTT (Augusta-specific, not generic)** — Course-specific putting knowledge matters. Generic tour putting metrics are weak predictors here. Players who've played Augusta often learn the greens in ways that don't show up in their broader putting stats.
[Claude] ### TIER STRUCTURE (Framework-based, no current data)
[Claude] **S-TIER — Elite Augusta Fit + Recent Pedigree**

[Claude] *Rory McIlroy* — The confirmed 2026 winner. Back-to-back champion. His APP game, distance profile, and now demonstrated Augusta mastery under pressure puts him in his own tier going forward. The "horror start / surrendered six-shot lead / still won" narrative actually reinforces his S-tier status — he doesn't need a perfect week to win here anymore. He knows how to win here in multiple ways now. Forward price will be enormous; value is a separate question for 2027.

[Claude] *Scottie Scheffler* — Without current form data I'm operating on framework knowledge, but Scheffler's APP metrics have been elite, his distance is adequate for Augusta, and his course management profile fits the Augusta model. He's the most complete ball-striker in the world when healthy. Augusta suits him structurally. If he's healthy and in form, he's S-tier.

[Claude] *Jon Rahm* — When Rahm is dialed in, his APP game is elite and he fits the Augusta course profile well. His distance is solid without being reckless. History at Augusta is strong. Health and form are the variables.
[Claude] **A-TIER — Strong Augusta Fit, One Legitimate Question Mark**

[Claude] *Collin Morikawa* — Ball-striking profile is built for Augusta. Iron play and approach precision are exactly what the angle-penalty course rewards. Putting at Augusta is the one question mark that keeps him out of S-tier for me.

[Claude] *Xander Schauffele* — Major pedigree is now established. APP game is strong. Distance is fine for Augusta. The question is whether Augusta specifically suits him or whether he's just a generically elite player.

[Claude] *Ludvig Åberg* — The young Swede's ball-striking profile is genuinely Augusta-appropriate. First-timer discount still applied depending on when this is, but if he's accumulated Augusta experience he moves up.

[Claude] *Tommy Fleetwood* — The Coley homepage snippet specifically names him as one of the Masters bets this week, which is worth noting even without full article context. Fleetwood's iron game is legitimately elite and Augusta fits his patient, precise profile. His putting has been the historic knock.
[Claude] *Viktor Hovland* — When the driver is working, his distance + APP combination works at Augusta. Inconsistency is the flag.
[Claude] **B-TIER — Legitimate Contenders with Course Fit Concerns or Form Questions**

[Claude] *Brooks Koepka* — Major specialist. Augusta history has been competitive. But his game profile leans more OTT-dominant than APP-dominant, which is a slight mismatch for Augusta's specific demands. He can win here but it's not his best fit.

[Claude] *Bryson DeChambeau* — Distance profile is obviously there. 13 and 15 are layups for him. But the precision demanded by the angle penalty works against his profile somewhat. Can absolutely contend but requires exceptional putting week.
[Claude] *Max Homa* — APP metrics have been strong in recent cycles. Augusta suits patient players. Putting has been the variable.
[Claude] *Shane Lowry* — Creative scrambler. ARG skills are legitimate. Augusta suits his patient, course-management style more than his raw metrics suggest.
[Claude] *Jordan Spieth* — Course history at Augusta is undeniable. Experience weight is real. But the game has had significant variance — needs current form data to calibrate properly.
[Claude] **C-TIER — Misfit or Significant Question Marks**

[Claude] Players whose primary skill is driving distance without precision, generic short-course specialists, first-timers without elite APP metrics, or players whose putting profile depends on generic Tour metrics rather than Augusta-specific course knowledge.
[Claude] **FADE — Active Avoids at Augusta**
[Claude] - Players whose SG total is driven primarily by putting spikes without corresponding APP strength (putting at Augusta is volatile and course-specific — generic putters don't replicate here)
[Claude] - First-time Augusta participants without elite ball-striking metrics
[Claude] - Players with high rough-penalty dependency in their game (Augusta's rough is LOW penalty, so these players don't get the full benefit of their fairway-finding discipline here)
[Claude] ### MARKET TRAP FLAGS (Structural, Augusta-specific)

[Claude] 🚩 **The Generic Putting Trap** — Every year the market over-weights players who are in a hot putting streak on Tour. Augusta's greens are not Tour greens. Course-specific putting knowledge accumulated over years matters more than current Tour SG:PUTT. If a player's DG win% is being inflated by a putting spike, apply serious skepticism at Augusta specifically.

[Claude] 🚩 **The Distance-Only Trap** — Yes, 13 and 15 reward distance. But the rest of the course has HIGH angle penalty. A pure bomber without precision approach skills is going to get hurt on the approach holes. Partial distance multiplier means distance matters but doesn't dominate.

[Claude] 🚩 **The First-Timer Narrative Trap** — Every year there's a hot young player who "fits Augusta perfectly" according to the metrics. Apply the newcomer discount. Not a fade, but not a top-tier play either. Augusta teaches lessons in person that no amount of data preparation covers.

[Claude] 🚩 **The Career Average Trap** — Augusta specifically. Career averages at Augusta are smaller samples than normal course history, and they mix prime years with off years. Recent form and recent Augusta rounds (last 2-3 visits) matter more than lifetime averages at this specific venue.
[Claude] ## 3. EARLY FRL TARGETS
[Claude] Without player names and without R1 historical data, I can only give you the profile of who scores well in Round 1 at Augusta:
[Claude] **R1 Augusta Profile — Who Historically Goes Low Early:**
[Claude] - Players with strong recent form entering the week (Augusta R1 rewards confidence)
[Claude] - Players in the 8-11am wave historically have benefited from softer morning conditions before wind picks up (wave assignments matter enormously here — flag for confirmation)
[Claude] - Experienced Augusta hands who play a "patient first round" strategy — they're not trying to shoot 63, they're trying to stay in the tournament and let others make mistakes
[Claude] - **Bombers on the par-5s** — 13 and 15 early birdies set the tone; players who can eagle these in R1 separate quickly
[Claude] - Players with hot recent APP form, not hot recent putting form — the greens take a round to read
[Claude] **Profile to AVOID for FRL:**
[Claude] - Debut participants (first-timers) — they're often figuring things out in R1
[Claude] - Players who need to "get going" — Augusta R1 punishes anxiety and aggression
[Claude] - Hot putters on Tour whose Augusta putting hasn't been established
[Claude] **Specific FRL Notes:**

[Claude] I need wave assignments and current form data before I get specific on FRL targets. This is the one bet type at Augusta where wave assignment is the single most important non-skill variable. Morning wave + calm conditions = significant R1 scoring advantage. I will not finalize FRL targets until wave assignments are confirmed.
[Claude] *Pool strategy is completely separate from the betting card. Odds are irrelevant here. I am picking who I think is most likely to WIN, full stop.*
[Claude] **Event Tier: MAJOR** *(Highest stakes pool event of the year)*
[Claude] **2026 RESULT (confirmed):** Rory McIlroy — Back-to-back Masters champion.
[Claude] Since this is a Sunday night debrief after the event has concluded, the pool section is primarily forward-looking framework for 2027.
[Claude] **PRIMARY POOL PICK (2027 Forward Framework):**

[Claude] *Scottie Scheffler* — If he's healthy, he's my primary pool pick going into 2027. The best ball-striker in the world when operating at full capacity, his profile fits Augusta's demands at every level. The only reason I don't put him above McIlroy right now is that McIlroy has now demonstrated he can win Augusta in multiple ways — when dominant, and when scrambling from a deficit. That's the mark of a course master.

[Claude] *Rory McIlroy* — He's the two-time defending champion. I can't fade him for pool. The history of Augusta three-peats is poor (no one has done it), but the market will price him as a heavy favorite and in pool strategy, if everyone else is avoiding him due to the three-peat narrative, he actually becomes MORE attractive as a pool play, not less.
[Claude] **Available 2027 Pool Plays (Tier Structure):**
[Claude] | Tier | Player | Pool Rationale |
[Claude] | Primary | Rory McIlroy | Defending x2, course master, demonstrated wins in multiple ways |
[Claude] | Primary | Scottie Scheffler | Best ball-striker alive, Augusta profile elite |
[Claude] | Strong | Collin Morikawa | Ball-striking built for Augusta, growing Augusta experience |
[Claude] | Strong | Ludvig Åberg | Profile fits, accumulating Augusta rounds |
[Claude] | Strong | Tommy Fleetwood | Iron game elite, Augusta suits his patience |
[Claude] | Speculative | Jordan Spieth | Experience weight is enormous, if form returns |
[Claude] | Speculative | Jon Rahm | Health-dependent but Augusta fit is real |
[Claude] **Trap Plays for Pool (Avoid):**
[Claude] - Any player whose primary selling point is "hot putter" entering the week — Augusta putting volatility makes this unreliable for a pool primary
[Claude] - First-time Masters participants regardless of metrics — newcomer discount is real
[Claude] - Players riding a OTT distance advantage without proven APP precision — partial distance multiplier means this isn't enough
[Claude] - Defending Open/US Open champions with no Augusta experience — major pedigree doesn't transfer here the way people think
[Claude] ## 5. FLAGS & UNCERTAINTIES
[Claude] **FLAG 1 — Complete Article Blackout (6 of 8 articles blocked/missing)**

[Claude] This is the most significant data gap I've ever had to work around. Six of eight primary analytical sources returned 403/404 errors. The two that processed returned no usable tournament content. I am operating almost entirely on framework knowledge and course parameters. This analysis should be treated as a structural framework document, not a current-form document. When current SG data, recent form, and proper article content is available, this entire analysis should be re-run.
[Claude] **FLAG 2 — Field Data Completely Missing**

[Claude] All 50 field rows returned as Unknown/N/A. I have no player names, no SG data, no DG win percentages, no designations. This makes the player-by-player analysis impossible in the standard format. I've substituted framework-based Augusta fit assessments using trained knowledge, but these MUST be validated against current data when available.
[Claude] **FLAG 3 — No Weather Data**
[Claude] Augusta weather is a meaningful variable — wind direction and speed affect which holes play hardest and which wave has the advantage. Cannot calibrate wave-based FRL analysis without this.
[Claude] **FLAG 4 — Post-Event Timing Ambiguity**

[Claude] The request is dated Monday, April 13 (after the final round Sunday, April 13), and the Coley snippet confirms the tournament is over with McIlroy winning. This is a debrief/forward-framework run, not a pre-event analysis. I've structured accordingly but flagging in case the intent was different.
[Claude] ### 🟡 CALIBRATION NOTES
[Claude] **CALIBRATION NOTE 1 — McIlroy "Horror Start + Surrendered Six-Shot Lead" Narrative**

[Claude] The Coley snippet is the only real content signal I have from this week. The detail that Rory surrendered a six-shot lead but still won is meaningfully important for future framework calibration. It suggests: (a) the
[Claude] ## ⚠️ CRITICAL DATA FLAGS — READ FIRST
[Claude] Before I go any further, I need to be completely transparent about what I'm working with here, because this affects everything downstream.

[Claude] **Field & Skill Data:** Every single player in the field came back as "Unknown / N/A" across all metrics — no player names, no SG data, no DG win percentages, no designations. That's a total data wipeout. I have zero individual player stat lines to work from.

[Claude] **Articles:** 12 of the 16 attempted articles were blocked (403/404). The 4 that processed were duplicates of two pieces — Bamford and Coley — and both came back as essentially navigation/promo pages with no actual Masters analysis content. The only substantive piece of information that came through was a headline from Sporting Life: **"McIlroy wins the Masters again"** — meaning Rory McIlroy has apparently just won the 2026 Masters Tournament, defending his title and becoming the fourth player in history to defend at Augusta.

[Claude] **Bottom line:** I'm running a post-tournament Sunday night analysis with no stat data, no player names, no pre-tournament articles, and no odds. What I *do* have is: Augusta National course knowledge, my framework, and one confirmed result headline. I'm going to give you everything I can within those constraints and be explicit about what's framework-grounded vs. what's inference.
[Claude] ## 1. BRIEFING PARAGRAPH

[Claude] Augusta National is the most unforgiving course on tour for punishing wrong answers. The Angle Penalty is HIGH for a reason — this is not a course where you spray it and recover. Every approach has a preferred window, and the players who win here repeatedly aren't just ball-strikers, they're *thinkers* who understand that laying up at 13 or 15 at the right moment is as valuable as going for it in the right moment. The dominant stat profile here is genuinely balanced — you need OTT quality to access the par-5s (13 and 15 in particular reward distance meaningfully), you need iron precision to hit the correct quadrants of these greens rather than just the putting surface, and you need Augusta-specific putting competency that doesn't translate from generic Tour SG:PUTT numbers. The rough penalty being LOW doesn't mean missing fairways is free — it means the penalty comes from angles and positions, not from vegetation. That's actually harder to model and easier to underestimate in prep.

[Claude] The confirmed result — Rory McIlroy defending his Masters title — is historically significant and framework-relevant. Defending at Augusta is one of the hardest things in golf. The course's premium on course knowledge and management experience is supposed to help veterans, but the field also has that same knowledge. What McIlroy apparently demonstrated over four rounds, including overcoming what sounds like a difficult start in the final round ("horror start" per the headline) and a six-shot lead he'd surrendered, is elite course management under pressure combined with the ball-striking engine to back it up. That's the Augusta profile working exactly as the framework predicts: T2G as the floor, clutch management as the ceiling. The fact that he clawed back after a horror start and still won tells me his APP game held up when it mattered — you don't recover at Augusta on putts alone.
[Claude] ## 2. INITIAL PLAYER RANKINGS
[Claude] ### ⚠️ CRITICAL LIMITATION

[Claude] With zero field data (all players returned "Unknown/N/A"), I cannot produce stat-based individual rankings. What I can do is articulate the **course-fit framework** that would drive those rankings, and anchor it to the one confirmed data point I have.
[Claude] ### AUGUSTA NATIONAL COURSE-FIT HIERARCHY (Framework Template)
[Claude] **The Winning Profile at Augusta:**

[Claude] 1. **Distance + control combination** — not just distance, not just control. The OTT profile that wins here is a bomber who can work the ball. Pure bombers who spray it get punished by angles. Pure technicians can't access the par-5s properly.

[Claude] 2. **APP elite in recent window** — this is the engine. The greens demand precise distance control and shape. Players who are gaining strokes from 100-175 yards with iron precision are rewarded here more than anywhere.

[Claude] 3. **Augusta putting specifically** — I never trust generic SG:PUTT at Augusta. The break here is unique, the speed is unique, the way putts behave on these Bermuda greens prepped to Augusta spec is course-specific. A player with +1.5 SG:PUTT on Tour might be +0.0 here. I discount Tour putting numbers meaningfully at this event.

[Claude] 4. **Experience and course knowledge** — I apply a meaningful discount to first-timers regardless of their metrics. Not a disqualifier, but real. Augusta rewards players who've played hundreds of practice rounds here and know every pin quadrant.

[Claude] 5. **Mental game and management** — this one can't be quantified. The leaderboard pressure at Augusta is different. Amen Corner changes people. Players who have cracked here before get a flag.
[Claude] ### TIER ASSIGNMENTS (Framework-Based, No Individual Data)
[Claude] **S-TIER — Would bet to win:**

[Claude] Profile: Elite T2G (APP + OTT), Augusta experience, recent form, course management reputation. Based on the confirmed result, McIlroy occupies this tier — defending champion, elite ball-striker, now demonstrably capable of winning here under pressure with a lead and without one.
[Claude] **A-TIER — Strong contender, price dependent:**
[Claude] Profile: Strong ball-striking metrics, multiple Augusta appearances, no obvious weakness that Augusta punishes. These are the players who should have been in contention Sunday.
[Claude] **B-TIER — Live longshot if metrics align:**

[Claude] Profile: One or two Augusta-specific strengths but a question mark somewhere. Maybe elite driver, average iron play. Or elite irons, limited Augusta experience. Interesting at the right price, not a primary target.
[Claude] **C-TIER — Round-by-round interest only:**
[Claude] Profile: Metrics don't fit the course type, limited experience, or recent form trending wrong direction.

[Claude] Profile: Generic putting spike driving SG total (market trap). Players whose T2G is mediocre but a hot putter has inflated their recent results. Augusta putts don't transfer from other courses. This is the primary market trap at this event every year.
[Claude] ### MARKET TRAP FLAGS (Standing Rules for Augusta)

[Claude] 🚨 **The Putter Trap** — Any player whose recent SG surge is primarily PUTT-driven should be faded hard at Augusta. The putting here is course-specific. Tour putting form is almost noise. If I were running full stat data, this would be my primary filter — find whoever the market is pricing up on a putting run and fade them.

[Claude] 🚨 **The Augusta First-Timer** — Elite DG ranking, never played Augusta competitively. No matter how good the metrics, I'm applying a 15-20% conceptual discount. The course creates specific problems you cannot prepare for without having experienced them.

[Claude] 🚨 **The Distance-Only Bomber** — Huge distance numbers, average-to-below APP. Augusta has long been misread as a pure bomber's paradise. It's a bomber's paradise *with precision*. The angle penalty is HIGH. Spraying it long is worse than hitting it straight and short.
[Claude] ## 3. EARLY FRL TARGETS
[Claude] ### Augusta R1 Historical Profile
[Claude] Round 1 at Augusta historically rewards players who:

[Claude] **1. Have played multiple Masters** — The first round at Augusta for veterans is almost mechanical. They know their yardages, they know the pin positions from Tuesday/Wednesday practice rounds, they're not seeing anything for the first time. Course comfort shows up most clearly in R1.

[Claude] **2. Play the par-5s efficiently** — Holes 2, 8, 13, 15. Gaining on par-5s in R1 while the field is still finding their footing is a consistent pattern. Players with the distance profile to go for 13 and 15 in two, combined with the approach precision to execute, separate early.

[Claude] **3. Avoid Amen Corner trouble** — 11, 12, 13 cluster. Players with strong course management histories at Augusta tend to navigate R1 without the big numbers that derail leaderboard position before you've even started.

[Claude] **4. Ball-striking in form entering the week** — Not hot putters. Ball-strikers. The greens in R1 are typically not at their fastest/firmest — that comes later in the week. R1 scoring is more about hitting good shots than making miraculous putts.

[Claude] **FRL Profile Summary:** Look for experienced Augusta players, strong APP metrics, par-5 access via distance, and recent iron form. Fade the player whose recent FRL track record is built on birdie barrages driven by a hot putter.
[Claude] *Note: Without individual player names and stats in the data, I cannot assign specific FRL targets. This section will need to be populated once odds and player data arrive Monday.*
[Claude] **Event Tier: MAJOR**
[Claude] *Note: Pool strategy operates completely separately from the betting card. Odds are irrelevant here. I'm picking who I think is most likely to win.*
[Claude] ### PRIMARY POOL PICK
[Claude] **Rory McIlroy** *(confirmed winner of the 2026 Masters)*

[Claude] This is post-tournament analysis — McIlroy won. The framework validation here is important: the defending champion won again, becoming only the fourth player in history to do so at Augusta. What this confirms for future pool strategy:
[Claude] - Experience weight at Augusta is not overrated in the framework
[Claude] - T2G engine players survive Augusta adversity (he overcame a horror start in R4)
[Claude] - The "clutch ceiling" element — hard to quantify, increasingly hard to ignore after two consecutive Augusta wins
[Claude] ### AVAILABLE PLAYS (Framework-Based Pool Guidance for Future Reference)

[Claude] **Use in pool:** Players with the full Augusta profile — distance + APP precision + multiple Augusta starts + strong recent form entering the week. McIlroy is now the consensus #1 pool play going forward until his form breaks.

[Claude] **Secondary pool targets:** Players who finished T5 or better and showed the correct ball-striking profile. Without leaderboard data I can't name them, but the framework says: whoever was in the final pairing or within 3 shots with 18 holes to play almost certainly has the Augusta-fit profile worth pooling going forward.
[Claude] ### TRAP PLAYS TO AVOID IN POOL
[Claude] 🚫 **First-time Augusta competitors** — No matter the ranking. Pool is about winning probability maximization. First-timers don't win here often enough to justify the pool slot.
[Claude] 🚫 **Players whose recent form is putting-dependent** — At Augusta specifically, this is a pool death trap. The putting doesn't transfer. The ball-striking does.

[Claude] 🚫 **Players with Augusta history of Amen Corner disasters** — Mental scar tissue is real. Some players just don't win here. If someone has blown up at 12 in multiple Masters, I'm not trusting them with a pool pick regardless of their metrics.

[Claude] 🚫 **Fading the defending champion going forward** — The framework would have flagged McIlroy as a pool play again next year. Course winners repeat here more than anywhere on tour. Two-time champions repeat even more.
[Claude] ## 5. FLAGS & UNCERTAINTIES
[Claude] **FLAG 1 — Total Field Data Failure**

[Claude] All 50 player slots returned "Unknown / N/A" across every metric. This is not a data gap — it's a complete data wipeout. This analysis cannot produce individual player assessments, stat-based rankings, or specific betting targets. Everything in this report is framework-based and course-architecture-based only. When odds and player data arrive Monday, the full analysis needs to be rebuilt from scratch.
[Claude] **FLAG 2 — Article Blockage (12 of 16)**

[Claude] Six sources were entirely inaccessible (Haslbauer, Steve Rawlings, Dave Tindall, Tom Jacobs, Sharpside/Gannon — all either 403 or 404). These are all high-weight sources in my framework (several are 9-10 weight). The two sources that did process (Bamford, Coley) came back as navigation pages with no substantive content. I am working with functionally zero external expert analysis.
[Claude] **FLAG 3 — Post-Tournament Timing Conflict**

[Claude] This is flagged as a Sunday Night pre-analysis, but the articles confirm the tournament has concluded (McIlroy wins headline, "Three Sunday takeaways" piece referenced). The field and odds data not yet arriving is unusual given the tournament is over. This may be a workflow timing issue where the analysis framework is running but the correct data feed hasn't populated yet. Recommend confirming whether this is meant to be a post-tournament debrief or if there's a data pipeline issue.
[Claude] **FLAG 4 — Wrong Event Articles**

[Claude] The Bamford and Coley articles that did process were titled "RBC Heritage Tips 2026" — meaning they were for *next week's event*, not the Masters. This is not useful for Masters analysis. The only Masters-relevant content was in a Sporting Life navigation snippet.
[Claude] ### 🟡 CALIBRATION NOTES
[Claude] **CALIBRATION NOTE 1 — McIlroy Defending**

[Claude] The framework historically applies a slight discount to defending champions because the field knows their game, they face elevated scrutiny/media pressure, and complacency is a real factor. Augusta specifically may be an exception to this rule. The course knowledge advantage for a defending champion here is more durable than at other events because the knowledge is so course-specific. I'd adjust the framework to apply a *smaller* defending champion discount at Augusta than at standard Tour events.
[Claude] **CALIBRATION NOTE 2 — "Horror Start" Recovery**

[Claude] The Sporting Life headline notes McIlroy overcame a "horror start" in R4. This is worth flagging as a framework data point: elite ball-strikers at Augusta can absorb early adversity and recover because the course has opportunities (par-5s) to make up ground in clusters. A player with a bad front nine isn't dead at Augusta the way they might be at a tight, penalizing course. This reinforces the T2G engine > putting spike framework specifically here.
[Claude] **CALIBRATION NOTE 3 — "Six-Shot Lead Surrendered"**

[Claude] McIlroy entered Sunday with a six-shot lead and surrendered it before winning. This is a significant narrative that I can't fully analyze without knowing the full leaderboard, but it confirms that Augusta specifically creates conditions where leads evaporate due to course chaos (Amen Corner, par-5 gamble decisions, etc.). For future betting: having a six-shot lead at Augusta entering Sunday is less safe than anywhere else. This is a live-betting calibration note.
[Claude] ### 🟢 PROCESS NOTES FOR MONDAY
[Claude] When data arrives Monday, here's the rebuild sequence:
[Claude] 1. **Get actual player field list with stats** — Every player, recent-window SG data (APP, OTT, PUTT, T2G), and DG win probabilities
[Claude] 2. **Run the market trap check** — Identify who's being priced on a putting run vs. a ball-striking run
[Claude] 3. **Apply experience filter** — Flag first-timers and first/second-timers for the newcomer discount
[Claude] 4. **Apply Augusta putting discount** — Reduce SG:PUTT weight by ~40% vs. standard event. Increase course history putting data weight if available
[Claude] 5. **Check the par-5 access question** — Who has the distance to go for 13 and 15 in two realistically? This is a legitimate differentiator
[Claude] 6. **Odds arrive → value tier assignments** — No picks without prices
[Claude] 7. **Final card Wednesday**

[Claude] *This analysis reflects the maximum available output given a complete field data failure and 12 blocked articles. The framework is intact. The data pipeline needs to be checked before Monday's full build.*
